does: This paper develops a systematic framework for diagnosing failures in Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) by introducing three new metrics to measure alignment quality. Feature imprint quantifies how strongly reward models learn to recognize desired target features versus undesired spoiler features through regression analysis of RM scores. Alignment resistance measures the proportion of preference data where reward models fail to match human preferences, identifying systematic inconsistencies. Alignment robustness evaluates how stable reward model judgments remain under input perturbations. The authors apply these metrics to existing open-source datasets and models, revealing substantial failures in learning target features and significant sensitivity to input variations that compromise alignment effectiveness.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature investigating RLHF's internal mechanisms, extending beyond prior work that treated reward models as black boxes. It responds to concerns raised by recent papers questioning whether RLHF truly captures human values or simply exploits dataset artifacts. The paper connects to broader research on mechanistic interpretability in language models and feature attribution methods, adapting regression-based analysis to the preference learning setting. It also relates to work on adversarial robustness in neural networks, applying perturbation analysis to understand reward model fragility in the alignment context. The systematic categorization of dataset features into target and spoiler components extends prior conceptualizations of what preference data actually teaches models.
enables: This framework enables more rigorous diagnosis and debugging of RLHF failures before deployment, allowing researchers to identify whether alignment breakdowns stem from insufficient feature learning, inconsistent preference annotation, or brittle reward models. The metrics provide quantitative targets for improving RLHF procedures and comparing different alignment approaches. The robustness analysis opens investigation into why reward models make contradictory judgments and how to build more stable alignment signals. Future work could use these diagnostics to design better preference datasets, improve reward model training procedures, or develop ensemble methods that mitigate identified failure modes. The framework also enables comparative studies across different RLHF implementations and datasets.
